data.title: FPF: Dividend Can Be Sustained But Not A Buy Yet
data.description:
Overview

Market indices continue to retreat from their all time highs due to uncertainty around the technology markets and how AI will impact businesses. While investors that are invested in traditional equities are dealing with this volatility, income investors may see greater success preserving their capital. This is where the First Trust Intermediate Duration Preferred (FPF) comes in, since I believe it can be a valuable long-term holding for an investor seeking stability. When I previously covered FPF, I issued a hold rating due to its inconsistent dividend coverage. However, the fund has released an updated annual report for 2025, which prompted me to revisit FPF's current value proposition now that we've crossed into the new year.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that FPF's share price has increased by about 2.1%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to recover from the prior year's market decline and provide resilience for investors. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to about 10.8% over the same time frame. FPF now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 8.7%, while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. Although the fund's earnings can wildly vary, I believe that dividend will remain unchanged for the next twelve months based on the latest annual report.

The latest annual report provides some confidence that the fund's portfolio strategy can remain resilient during a scenario where we see heightened market volatility and uncertainty. Despite the appeal, there are still some flaws and tradeoffs that investors should consider before initiating a position. For instance, FPF isn't structurally capable of participating in market rallies, which is likely to lead to underperformance over a longer holding period. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that FPF implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, FPF has total managed assets of ~$1.8B that are spread across a range of preferred and other income-focused securities. The fund has a listed expense ratio of 4.20%, but this is misleading because it includes fees related to interest and other operating costs. Since FPF is structured as a closed-end fund, it is legally required to bulk in these expenses but this isn't the actual cost for investors. When excluding these other costs, the actual management fee sits at 1.30% of net assets.

FPF essentially takes a blended approach with its portfolio between preferred securities and a blend of debt securities to help produce consistent income through different market cycles. The fund has a primary goal of providing a high current income and achieves this by allocating most of its portfolio to bank related securities at 49.43% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to insurance and capital markets, which accounts for 13.69% and 8.30% of assets respectively. Despite a sense of diversity, it is clear that FPF may present a greater concentration risk to the financial sector. So if financial companies face any specific headwind, I would anticipate FPF to be heavily impacted.

Furthermore, FPF is capable of providing such a high yield because the majority of its portfolio is allocated to securities with undesirable credit ratings. For reference, securities with a credit rating below a BBB are classified as below investment grade. These are borrowers that present a greater risk of being impacted by defaults during times of higher interest rates. For reference, here is the fund's breakdown by credit rating:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 44.72%.
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 51.13%
  • Not Rated: 4.15%

When looking at the top holdings of the fund, we can see that FPF provides exposure to many high quality business. For instance, Bank of America (BAC) is the largest holding at 3.20% of assets. This is followed by varying allocations to other notable names, such as Barclays (BCS), Wells Fargo (WFC), HSBC Holdings (HSBC), and BNP Paribas (BNPQF), just to name a few. We can see that each of these positions is being held through an income instrument, rather than common shares.

Updated Valuation & Performance Insights

At the time of my last coverage, FPF traded at a discount to NAV of 2.87%. Although the share price has remained resilient since then, FPF now trades at a slightly smaller discount to NAV of 4.69%. For reference, FPF has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.69% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that FPF doesn't exactly trade at the most attractive valuation, but it sits within a reasonable price in relation to its historical range.

Since my last coverage, FPF has released an updated annual report for the full year of 2025. This is the most updated report that provides transparency into the fund's earnings. Net investment income amounted to $1.21 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.76 per share. Looking back on prior years, FPF's net investment income continues to stay on par with its typical range. This is an indication that the portfolio is efficient at generating income through different market conditions. However, net investment income hasn't historically been large enough to support distributions on its own.

Therefore, FPF is reliant on net realized gains to bridge that gap in earnings. The issue is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions and this makes it difficult to predict FPF's earnings over time. For instance, market indices increased to their all-time highs in 2025, but FPF only experienced net realized gains of $0.76 per share, which undercut its prior year's net realized gains of $3.46 per share. This random nature of earnings can disrupt a steady flow of NAV growth over time. However, FPF has been able to regain positive NAV momentum and is working its way back to its pre-pandemic levels. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $24.93 per share
  • 2022: $18.47 per share
  • 2023: $16.77 per share
  • 2024: $19.70 per share
  • 2025: $20.02 per share

Since the fund is reliant on net realized gains to partially support distributions, this means that FPF has to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions. Combine this with the fact that FPF has no equity exposure, the fund's share price has provided minimal capital appreciation over a longer holding period. For instance, FPF's share price has actually declined by about 6.3% over the last decade. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 106.4% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that nearly all of their returns will come from the fund's ability to provide a high rate of distributions over time.

Therefore, I want to put emphasis on the fact that a position in FPF is very defensive in nature and may not be a good fit for investors seeking to maximize total returns. This means that the fund may be best aligned for investors that are at or nearing the retirement age and want to create a supplemental income stream. However, there are some additional risks that should be considered. For instance, FPF's high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to high returns or resilience over time.

During periods of market declines, the fund will also experience declines and may see net realized losses. Therefore, FPF may find itself in a scenario where it is paying out more than it actually earned in income, which can lead to capital erosion and greater losses than the indices. For instance, markets declined in 2022 due to uncertainty around the pandemic, fluctuating interest rates, and a shifting labor market. The S&P 500 (SPY) saw a total loss of 18.17% for the year. Despite FPF offering a higher yield, the fund failed to generate earnings that actually supported the distributions. As a result, FPF experienced a larger total loss of 25.8% through 2022.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1375 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.7%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of about $1.65 per share. Referring to the updated annual report for 2025, we can see that FPF's dividend coverage has improved, although it is inconsistent. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $3.80 per share
  • 2022: ($4.96) per share
  • 2023: ($0.39) per share
  • 2024: $4.55 per share
  • 2025: $1.97 per share

Although performance was poor during 2022 and 2023, I believe this can be forgiven considering the market's reaction to the pandemic at that time. During FPF's stronger performing years, the fund demonstrated its ability to generate earnings that were capable of supporting several years worth of payouts. So despite the variable nature of earnings, I believe that FPF will likely sustain its current payout over the next twelve months.

2024's earnings of $4.55 per share / $1.65 annual payout = 2.75 years.

Lastly, investors need to consider that the distributions received from FPF may not offer any tax-efficiency. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 82.27% of distributions paid YTD were supported by net investment income, which has the highest tax consequences for investors. Therefore, a position in FPF would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that FPF is still a hold for existing shareholders that want to prioritize a reliable stream of dividend income. However, I do not see any compelling catalyst in the near-term that makes FPF a buy yet. The fund trades at a reasonable price to NAV valuation, but I would like to see a deeper discount to NAV level before I'd consider accumulating. The fund's latest earnings instills confidence that FPF will be able to sustain its current payouts for the next twelve months, since the earnings have produced a lot of excess income. However, I would like to see net investment income levels improved so that the fund's reliance on net realized gains is reduced.

data.title: The 1-Minute Market Report, February 15, 2026
data.description:
Two weeks ago I wrote an article called “The Market Bubble Is About To Burst.” It caused quite a stir among my subscribers and followers because the title made it sound as if I had turned fully bearish. But this is not the case. Bubbles can continue to inflate for longer than one would expect, and today is no exception. However, the signs of complacency that I see are causing me to be more concerned than I have been recently, which is why I wrote the article.

Looking ahead, my bear market probability model puts the odds of a bear market in the next 4-6 months at 25%. That’s not an alarming number, but it’s not zero either. Caution is advised, and it wouldn’t hurt to raise a little more cash just to be safe.

S&P 500 Last 4 Weeks

The chart below shows how the market started out strong but couldn’t keep the momentum going. Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday were all down days, and they wiped out the gains we got on Monday and Friday. The net result was a decline of 1.4% for the week.

Zoom Out to 12 Months

This chart shows how big of a hole we’re digging for ourselves so far this month. 1.5% isn’t much from a historical perspective, and it’s possible that we could make up that deficit in a single trading day. What I’m looking for next week is whether the optimists will show up to buy the dip, as they have been doing for more than 3 years now.

S&P 500 Drawdowns

This chart puts last week’s decline in perspective. As long as investors hold the line at minus 5%, we can continue to make more new highs this year.

A Look at the Bull Run Since 2022

This chart shows the 91% gain in the S&P 500 since the bull market began in 2022. After last week’s decline, we are resting right on the long-term regression line.

Major Index Performance Last Week

This chart confirms that the rotation away from U.S. stocks and into foreign stocks is still happening. The biggest loser among major indices was the NASDAQ 100, which is where the Mag 7 live.

Major Asset Class Performance

Non-US stocks and US REITs were the best performers last week, while U.S. stocks and blockchain companies sold off.

Equity Sector Performance

Utilities had an outstanding week—up 7%. They outperformed all other sectors by a mile, while Financials were the source of funds.

Factor Returns

Investors turned to dividend-paying stocks for the third week in a row. Could this be another durable trend? It’s too early to tell, but it has the potential to become one.

Growth vs. Value Returns

Value stocks dominated last week, continuing the theme of bargain hunting in an expensive market.

Commodities Returns

Gold managed to bounce back slightly from the prior two-week selloff, but silver continued its February slide. To put this in perspective, silver hit a high of $121 at the end of January, and now it changes hands for $77/Oz. That’s a decline of 35% in two weeks. But silver wasn’t the worst performer recently. That honor goes to Bitcoin, which hit the 50% decline level before bouncing back up about 5% last week.

Non-U.S. Markets

Japan was the top-performing foreign market again last week, while Hong Kong stocks sold off. On a YTD basis, S. Korea is still in first place. Indonesia is in last place.

Equity Group Performance

This table captures the flow of big money last week. It was another very bad week for investors who hold large positions in the Mag 7 stocks. The exception was Tesla (TSLA), which gained 1.5% on the week.

In a down week, you would expect defensive stocks in sectors like utilities and consumer staples to see an influx of buying, and that’s exactly what happened last week. The rotation away from large caps and into smaller companies is a continuing trend this year.

The S&P Mag 7+

Here is a look at the seven, eight mega-cap stocks that have been leading the market over the past few years. These eight stocks have become a drag on the performance of the S&P 500.

The biggest loser last week was Apple (AAPL), down 8% and giving back what it had gained the prior week.

The S&P Top 7+ Share of Gains

The S&P 500 index is down 0.1% this year, but most stocks are up slightly. It’s the Mag 7 that are dragging the index down.

The Ten Best-Performing ETFs Last Week

Gold miners bounced back with the rebound in gold prices. One of the ETFs I’m watching closely is ITB, the home construction fund. It’s having a good run lately, and if that continues, I think it bodes well for the economy in general.

The Ten Worst-Performing ETFs YTD

It has gotten to the point where Cannabis companies seem to be a permanent resident of the worst-performing ETF list.

The Ten Best-Performing Momentum Stocks Year-to-Date

Here are the 10 best-performing stocks in the S&P 1500 on a year-to-date basis. Sandisk (SNDK) was the top momentum stock for the third week in a row. Could Sandisk be headed for a fall, like silver has? Maybe, and that is why I wouldn’t buy it at these prices. I would rather wait and buy it after it stabilizes and begins to move back up.

Final Thoughts

Halfway through February, and we have reset the scoreboard to zero. I have an indicator that compares the number of up days to down days during the first 30 trading days of the year. When there are more up days, the market ends the year higher 88% of the time. This year the indicator counted 16 up days out of the first 30, so that’s one bullish data point out of many that I track.

To Recap the Last Week’s Action, Investors Were:

  • Selling the Mag 7 and buying the other 493 stocks in the S&P 500
  • Selling US stocks and buying foreign stocks
  • Selling blockchain and buying REITs
  • Selling Financials and buying Utilities
  • Selling Growth and buying Value
  • Selling Silver and buying Gold
  • Selling AAPL and buying TSLA
  • Selling Hong Kong and buying Japan and S. Korea

data.title: DTD: Why Rotation Trade Favors Dividend Payers In 2026
data.description:
Rotation and diversification have been the market’s passwords in recent months. So far in 2026, yield plays and value stocks have outperformed the precisely flat S&P 500 (YTD through Valentine’s Day). One fund that has benefited from money moving out of the Mag 7 and into blue-chip dividend payers is the WisdomTree US Total Dividend Fund ETF (DTD).

I had a buy rating on the ETF back in December of 2024. Shares have returned 16% since then, outperforming the S&P 500. Today, the valuation is not as compelling, but the chart is on a clear uptrend. I reiterate a buy rating.

Value Stocks Gain Ground on Tech

In the Last 68 Years, There Were Only Six Years Where Dividend Levels Declined, and Only One Where They Fell More Than 5%

According to the issuer, DTD offers investors exposure to core US all-cap equity from a broad range of dividend-paying companies, and the ETF can be used to complement or replace all-cap value or dividend-oriented active and passive strategies. The fund seeks to track the performance of the WisdomTree U.S. Dividend Index through representative sampling.

DTD has grown just modestly since my previous assessment. It’s a somewhat small fund, now with $1.55 billion in assets under management, up from $1.4 billion last time. The annual expense ratio is low at 28 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is 1.88%, more than half a percentage point above that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum is very strong, rated a B+ by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. DTD also boasts solid risk characteristics, with a diversified portfolio and rather modest historical annualized standard deviation metrics.

Finally, liquidity looks good. Average daily volume may not be high (near 30,000 shares), but the median 30-day bid/ask spread is decently tight at 13 basis points, per WisdomTree Funds.

For an update on the portfolio, the 4-star, Gold-rated ETF by Morningstar has high exposure to the upper-left corner of the style box. Indeed, large-cap value is the predominant allocation type, and there is just 11% access to the growth side of the box. From a diversification perspective, SMID camps comprise almost one-third of the product, but there is essentially zero non-US exposure.

Today, the price-to-earnings ratio is not at a dirt-cheap level at 18.0x, but it’s more than three turns less expensive than the S&P 500’s earnings multiple. Pair that P/E with a long-term earnings growth rate of 8.8%, and the resulting PEG ratio is a somewhat neutral 2.0x.

DTD: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Looking closer at the sector breakdown, we find that Financials and Information Technology are far and away the largest positions. Now, on the surface, that may seem problematic, as those are two of the worst-performing S&P 500 sectors thus far in 2026. Again, though, the value and dividend focus has helped to weather some market storms YTD. An overweight to Energy has certainly been a boon, but Materials (the second-best SPX sector YTD) is an underweight.

Income investors may find the portfolio positioning helpful in that no single stock commands more than 3% of DTD.

DTD: Holdings & Dividend Information

Tech & Financials Among the Weakest SPX Sectors YTD

Seasonally, February and March have tended to be a tough stretch. So far, DTD is bucking that trend. Still, I would urge investors to be on guard for bouts of volatility over the next several weeks.

DTD: Soft Near-Term Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With shares a bit pricier on a P/E basis today versus late 2024, DTD’s technical situation is very strong. But is it too strong? Notice in the chart below that the fund turned nearly parabolic compared to its rise-and-grind trend that I offered color to some 14 months ago. What’s more, the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, confirming that the bulls control the primary trend.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It turned from trending lower throughout much of last year’s second half to moving upward. So, the price climb is confirmed by strong momentum, which I like to see.

Support is down at the $85 mark—where DTD paused in early Q4 2025. On the upside, $94 could be in play based on the depth of the November 2024 to April 2025 decline and subsequent breakout.

DTD: Bullish Uptrend, Rising 200dma, RSI Confirmation

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on DTD. The US large-cap dividend-payer ETF has solid fundamental and technical indicators right now. The market has been favoring value names, and I expect that trend to persist.

data.title: Globalstar: Commercial Execution Is Heating Up With New Contracts
data.description:
In my first cover of Globalstar (GSAT) in January 2025, I built a case arguing for Globalstar to be undervalued, and I issued a Strong Buy recommendation. Since that article, Globalstar is up 104%. You can read the article here. I argued that high margin expansion of the Internet of Things, commercialization of XCOM RAN, and the partnership with Apple are the primary catalysts for the stock.

In this article, I will evaluate the milestones Globalstar has achieved since I first wrote about them and how they are performing in their business areas and compare them to my earlier estimates. Due to the significant stock price appreciation, I am adjusting my rating for the medium term from Strong Buy to Buy. However, for the long term, I remain convinced that the stock is a Strong Buy.

XCOM RAN

A core part of my earlier coverage was XCOM RAN. In it, I noted that it enables high-capacity private 5G networks that work on Globalstar's dedicated Band n53 mid-band spectrum. My presumption was that this would play well with the AI-mediated automatization trend that we are currently seeing. I believe GSAT is still perfectly positioned to take advantage of this, and the recent events validate this. For a detailed description of how XCOM RAN works, I suggest reading my previous article.

Since I last wrote about GSAT, there have been 3 developments I’d like to talk about:

Warehouse Automation

In Q3, the company announced the completion of a new supply agreement and receipt of an initial order from a new warehouse automation customer. Although Globalstar didn’t specify who the customer is, the company has previously conducted tests with certain customers, and now we have a signed initial order. I think it is likely that this contract stems from one of those prior collaborations. This validates my theory from the previous article that customers see value in XCOM RAN. I believe that this initial order will result in broader commercial rollout and new contracts. This should provide a bullish catalyst for the stock.

Skydio Partnership

In December 2025, Globalstar and Skydio announced a partnership in the field of autonomous drones. The companies ran a trial that integrated Globalstar’s Band n53 spectrum directly into Skydio’s X10 drones using the XCOM RAN private 5G platform. Note that Skydio is not a niche player but a supplier for the US government.

I believe Skydio's choosing GSAT gives the company another vertical for expansion. Additionally, we have seen that the US military is increasingly relying on drones, and Globalstar is well-positioned to take advantage of this trend.

Boingo Wireless

In February 2026, Globalstar announced a partnership with Boingo Wireless. As stated in the agreement, ''Boingo will integrate XCOM RAN into its private network infrastructure. Boingo’s private networks deliver secure, high-bandwidth connectivity for critical operations at airports, transit hubs, stadiums, hospitals, convention centers, military bases, and more.'' This will, in my opinion, provide Globalstar high-margin revenue, which will likely resemble SaaS-based revenue. In the agreement, it was stated that ''Boingo’s leadership is built on experience—supporting more than 130 major airports and transportation hubs, 70+ stadiums and arenas, and 100 military bases.''

I believe that this will be a huge asset for Globalstar if they manage to execute well. With the Boingo deal, Globalstar’s XCOM RAN is now being used in industrial, public, and military applications. Also, I would like to point out that all these announced contracts were recent; thus, I estimate that 2026 revenues are poised to rise sharply, potentially pushing the price higher.

IoT Segment

In Q3 2025, Globalstar noted that the hardware sales increased 60% YoY. This was due to the release of the RM200M, which enables bi-directional communications. Previous hardware enabled only one-way data transmission; they were used basically as trackers. These new RM200M modules, however, also allow commands to be relayed to the specific hardware they control. For example, a module could be programmed to turn off a generator. We have seen how Palantir’s revenue is accelerating as they enable 2-way communication and automation within networks. The RM200M hardware provides similar command-and-control functions for remote assets

In my opinion, equipment sales are a leading indicator of the future subscription revenues, which I believe will provide the company with high-margin revenues.

Apple Partnership and C3 Constellation

Globalstar plans to launch C3 satellites via SpaceX in the first half of 2026, though we have not yet received specific launch dates. I believe the company will provide an update on the matter in the Q4 earnings call. For those unfamiliar with the matter, these are the upgraded satellites for which Apple has already agreed to reimburse 95% of the CapEx. Feel free to read the previous article where I went into greater detail regarding the partnership.

Valuation

I firmly believe Globalstar will beat analysts projected growth of 12% due to the soaring hardware sales numbers and recently announced XCOM RAN deals. In my opinion it is more reasonable to assume sales growth of 15-20%. Considering this, 2026 revenues would be approx. $335 million.

Globalstar had an EBITDA margin in the latest quarter approx. 50%. At this margin, estimated EBITDA in 2026 would be $168 million. Since I believe most of the revenues will be high-margin, subscription-based, an EV/EBITDA multiple of 30x seems reasonable. In this case, fair value would be $5.04 billion.

As I explained in the previous article, Band n53 should be worth about $6 billion. If we sum both parts, total valuation should be $11 billion. Globalstar's current market cap is $7.6 billion.

Additionally, I'd like to point out the company has a strong balance sheet. They have $401 million in current assets, while total liabilities stand at approx. $1.8 billion. I believe this is more than enough cash for further growth, especially considering that Apple refunds 95% of the infrastructure costs.

Risks

I believe the main risk is the delay in the launch of the C3 satellite constellation. Since GSAT outsources both manufacturing and launching to MDA Space, Rocket Lab, and SpaceX, any unexpected issues in their supply chain or technical issues will negatively impact Globalstar. Any delays will negatively impact the stock price.

Companies like Iridium (IRDM) are aggressively trying to expand in the IoT space. Still, I believe the sector is large, and with AI and automation, it is poised to further grow, supporting multiple winners. Additionally, as discussed in the article, Globalstar recently announced new contracts in the XCOM RAN vertical, and the IoT hardware revenues rose 60% YoY. This is concrete evidence that Globalstar is executing well.

Plan

Even though I am very bullish over the long term, I have trimmed my position to lock in profits. With the stock rising from $20 in mid-2025 to the current price of around $60, I believe we might see some pullback to the $45-$50 range. In my opinion, some pullback would be healthy, but the stock might also continue to rise. As I emphasized throughout the article, the stock remains undervalued in the long term. If a pullback occurs, I plan to accumulate shares to restore my full position.

data.title: Walmart To Lead Group Of 11 Companies Announcing Annual Dividend Increases In Second Half Of February
data.description:
This is the latest in my series of articles where I provide predictions of annual dividend increases for long-term dividend growth companies. At the end of January, I provided predictions for 19 dividend growth companies that have historically announced annual payout increases in the first half of February. In this article I’ll look at another 11 dividend growth companies that I expect will announce their annual dividend increases in the second half of February.

Before we see how my predictions from the first half of February fared (you can see the original predictions here) and my predictions for the second half of February, there were two companies that I missed:

- Paint company Sherwin-Williams (SHW) announced that it will recommend a 1.3% dividend increase to an annualized $3.20, giving the company a forward yield of 0.87%. If approved, this will be the company’s 48th year of dividend growth.

- Data center REIT Prologis (PLD) announced a 5.9% dividend boost for its 13th year of dividend growth. The new annual dividend of $4.28 gives the company a forward yield of 3.08%.

And here’s one honorable mention: while not technically a dividend growth stock (it skipped its increase last year), The Hershey Company (HSY) announced a 6.0% increase to an annualized $5.808. The increase, its 17th in the last 16 years, gives the candy company a forward yield of 2.61%. The company has paid dividends since at least 1930. I recommended the stock for income investors in a recent technical analysis article. You can see that article here.

(All yields are based on stock prices at the market close on Friday, February 13th.)

Results for Dividend Increase Announcements in the First Half of February

Analog Devices, Inc. (ADI)—23 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 10.1–12.1% increase to $4.36–$4.44

Actual: Deferred until the 2nd half of February

The semiconductor company should announce its next annual dividend boost when it reports earnings on February 17th.

Archer-Daniels-Midland Company (ADM)—51 years

Prediction: 1.0–2.9% increase to $2.06–$2.10

Actual: 2.0% increase to $2.08

Forward yield: 2.99%

As expected, the agricultural supplier announced another 4-cent annual dividend increase.

Allegion plc (ALLE)—13 years

Prediction: 6.9–9.8% increase to $2.18–$2.24

Actual: 7.8% increase to $2.20

Forward yield: 1.23%

Dividend growth at the security company accelerated from last year, but the announcement is still below the 5-year dividend growth rate of 11%.

Avista Corporation (AVA)—24 years

Prediction: 4.1–5.1% increase to $2.04–$2.06

Actual: 0.5% increase to $1.97

Forward yield: 4.57%

The electric and natural gas utility extends its dividend growth streak with a penny-per-share annual increase.

Cisco Systems, Inc. (CSCO)—16 years

Prediction: 2.4–4.9% increase to $1.68–$1.72

Actual: 2.4% increase to $1.68

Forward yield: 2.19%

The networking company continues to focus on share buybacks, having repurchased $1.4 billion in stock in the most recent quarter. Dividend growth is taking a back seat.

Quest Diagnostics Incorporated (DGX) – 16 years

Prediction: 5.6–6.9% increase to $3.38–$3.42

Actual: 7.5% increase to $3.44

Forward yield: 1.66%

Dividend growth at the medical diagnostics and testing company accelerated slightly from last year’s increase.

Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD)—11 years

Prediction: 5.1–7.6% increase to $3.32–$3.40

Actual: 3.8% increase to $3.28

Forward yield: 2.12%

Biopharmaceutical company Gilead breaks its streak of 8-cent annual increases with this year’s 12-cent boost.

Genuine Parts Company (GPC)—69 years

Prediction: 1.9–2.9% increase to $4.20–$4.24

Actual: Deferred until late February

The auto parts company will announce its 70th year of dividend growth when it announces earnings results before the market opens on Tuesday, February 17th.

ITT Inc. (ITT)—14 years

Prediction: 4.0–6.0% increase to $1.46–$1.49

Actual: 10.3% increase to $1.544

Forward yield: 0.76%

The industrial engineering manufacturer returned to double-digit dividend growth after last year’s 1% increase.

Jack Henry & Associates, Inc. (JKHY)—36 years

Prediction: 5.2–6.9% increase to $2.44–$2.48

Actual: 5.2% increase to $2.44

Forward yield: 1.56%

It’s another year of 5% dividend growth for the financial technology company.

The Coca-Cola Company (KO)—63 years of dividend growth

Prediction: 2.9–3.9% increase to $2.10–$2.12

Actual: Deferred until the 2nd half of February

Investors will have to wait until late February for Coca-Cola’s next dividend increase.

NextEra Energy, Inc. (NEE)—32 years

Prediction: 6.6–9.3% increase to $2.42–$2.48
